content.text: Standard Agile retrospective formats — Start/Stop/Continue, Mad/Sad/Glad — were designed for software delivery teams working with deterministic systems. GenAI delivery adds three dimensions those formats miss: output quality is probabilistic and only measurable through eval suites, failure modes are often subtle (model drift, prompt regressions, hallucinations) rather than binary, and improvement hypotheses require experiments rather than simple process changes. This sub-subtopic covers four retrospective formats adapted for these constraints.

attrs.alt: Four GenAI retrospective formats: Sprint Retro (biweekly, 4Ls format), Quality Retro (monthly, data-led), Architecture Retro (quarterly, pre-mortem), and Safety Retro (after incidents, blameless timeline + 5-why)

content.text: Why Standard Formats Fall Short

The core problem with applying standard retro formats to GenAI delivery is that team observations about "what went well" or "what slowed us down" cannot surface quality degradations that were not perceptible during the sprint. A prompt change that subtly reduced eval score by 1.5% will not appear in anyone's "what went wrong" list — but the quality retro, driven by the eval dashboard, will catch it.

GenAI retrospectives need data as a first-class input, not an optional supplement. Opening with "what did we observe this sprint?" before showing the eval chart produces a different (and less accurate) set of topics than opening with the chart first.

A second difference is that improvement actions in GenAI often require experiments rather than process changes. The action item from a standard retro is "we will do X differently." The action item from a GenAI quality retro is "we will run experiment Y to test hypothesis Z, and make a decision based on the eval result." This distinction affects how action items are written and tracked.
Format 1: Sprint Retrospective (Biweekly)
The sprint retro is the team's primary continuous improvement mechanism. It covers delivery process, not quality outcomes — the quality retro handles those separately.
The 4Ls Format for GenAI Teams
The 4Ls (Liked, Learned, Lacked, Longed For) works well for GenAI sprints because "Learned" explicitly captures experiment outcomes — a first-class activity in GenAI delivery:
Liked:
What worked well in the sprint? Include eval improvements, successful canary deployments, effective tooling.
Learned:
What did we discover about the system or our process? Include model behavior observations, prompt engineering insights, tooling findings.
Lacked:
What was missing that would have helped? Include eval tooling gaps, unclear acceptance criteria, data access blockers.
Longed For:
What do we wish we had? Include infrastructure capabilities, model features, monitoring improvements.
Data to Review Before the Sprint Retro
Open the sprint retro by reviewing three data sources before collecting observations:

## Sprint Retro Data Review (5 minutes before discussion)

### 1. Eval Score Trend
- Current score vs sprint start score: +/- ?
- Any fitness function BLOCK or WARN events this sprint?
- Any score changes that were not explained by a prompt commit?

### 2. Prompt / Config Change Log
- Number of prompt changes this sprint: N
- Changes that required eval reruns: N
- Changes that triggered a fitness WARNING: N

### 3. Deployment Friction
- Time from PR merge to production this sprint: avg N hours
- Rollbacks triggered: N
- On-call pages related to AI behavior: N

Review these numbers before anyone speaks. They anchor the discussion
to observable data rather than recency bias.
Sprint Retro Action Item Format
Every action item from a sprint retro must be specific enough to be verifiable at the next retro:

## Action Item Template

**What:** [Specific change or experiment]
**Why:** [What problem it solves, tied to a data point from the review]
**Owner:** [Single person — not "the team"]
**Done when:** [Observable, verifiable outcome]
**Due:** [Date — must be before next sprint retro]

## Examples of good action items

✓ Add a fitness function for p95 latency to the CI pipeline.
  Owner: @alice. Done when: fitness gate blocks any PR that increases p95 > 20%.
  Due: 2025-07-15.

✓ Investigate why eval score dropped 0.8% after commit a3f4b2.
  Owner: @bob. Done when: root cause identified and documented in the commit thread.
  Due: 2025-07-10.

## Examples of bad action items

✗ "Improve our eval process" — no specific change, no owner, not verifiable.
✗ "Be more careful with prompt changes" — behavior change, not system change.
✗ "Team to look into latency" — no single owner.
Format 2: Quality Retrospective (Monthly)
The quality retro is a monthly 90-minute session focused entirely on output quality trends. It is driven by data, not by team observations.
Structure
Data review (20 min).
Walk through the 30-day eval score chart, user feedback themes, and false positive/negative rate trends. The facilitator presents the data; discussion is deferred until step 2.
Hypothesis generation (25 min).

Based on the data, the team generates hypotheses explaining what they observe. Each hypothesis is written as a testable statement: "We believe X is causing Y, and running experiment Z will confirm or refute it."
Experiment prioritization (20 min).
Score each hypothesis by expected quality impact and experiment cost. Prioritize the top 2–3 for the next sprint.
Eval set review (15 min).
Review the eval set for coverage gaps. Are there failure categories from user feedback that the eval set does not cover? Add examples.
Action items (10 min).
Convert the prioritized experiments and eval additions into Jira tickets before the session ends.
Quality Retro Data Package

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date, timedelta

@dataclass
class QualityRetroPackage:
    period_start: date
    period_end: date

    # Eval trends
    eval_score_start: float
    eval_score_end: float
    eval_score_min: float
    eval_score_min_date: date
    fitness_warn_count: int
    fitness_block_count: int

    # User feedback
    thumbs_down_count: int
    thumbs_down_top_themes: list[str]
    support_ticket_count: int
    support_top_themes: list[str]

    # Eval set
    eval_set_size: int
    eval_set_last_updated: date
    new_failure_categories: list[str]  # from user feedback not yet in eval

    def summary(self) -> str:
        score_delta = self.eval_score_end - self.eval_score_start
        return (
            f"Period: {self.period_start} — {self.period_end}\n"
            f"Eval score: {self.eval_score_start:.3f} → {self.eval_score_end:.3f} "
            f"({score_delta:+.3f})\n"
            f"Lowest point: {self.eval_score_min:.3f} on {self.eval_score_min_date}\n"
            f"Fitness events: {self.fitness_warn_count} warnings, "
            f"{self.fitness_block_count} blocks\n"
            f"User thumbs-down: {self.thumbs_down_count}\n"
            f"Top feedback themes: {', '.join(self.thumbs_down_top_themes[:3])}\n"
            f"Eval set: {self.eval_set_size} examples "
            f"(last updated {self.eval_set_last_updated})\n"
            f"Coverage gaps: {', '.join(self.new_failure_categories) or 'none identified'}"
        )
Format 3: Architecture Retrospective (Quarterly)

The architecture retro is a quarterly 2-hour session that assesses whether the system's architecture is still fit for purpose and whether past architectural decisions are producing the outcomes that motivated them.
Pre-mortem as the Core Exercise

The most effective format for the architecture retro is a pre-mortem: "Imagine it is three months from now and the system has suffered a significant architectural failure. What failed, and what did we fail to address today that caused it?"

This format surfaces architectural risks that a retrospective-only discussion would miss, because it forces participants to think forward rather than backward. It is particularly useful for GenAI systems where risks are often slow-burning (model drift, eval debt, accumulating prompt debt) rather than sudden.
Architecture Retro Agenda

## Architecture Retrospective — Q[N] [YEAR]

### 1. ADR review (30 min)
For each ADR from the past quarter:
- Did the outcome match the intent?
- Are the reversal criteria still valid?
- Should any ADR be updated based on what we learned?

### 2. Fitness function audit (20 min)
- Are current fitness functions catching the regressions we care about?
- Are any fitness functions producing false positives that teams are ignoring?
- What architectural properties are NOT covered by any fitness function?

### 3. Debt register review (20 min)
- What debt items have grown in severity since last quarter?
- What debt items were resolved? Did resolution match the expected effort?
- Are there new debt categories not yet in the register?

### 4. Pre-mortem (30 min)
"It is Q[N+1] and we had a major architectural failure. What failed?"
- Generate failure scenarios (5 min silent writing, then share)
- Score scenarios by likelihood × impact
- Top 2 scenarios become architectural risk action items

### 5. Stage evolution assessment (10 min)
- Are any triggers for the next architecture stage currently active?
- If yes: begin drafting the evolution plan.
- If no: confirm current stage is appropriate.

### 6. Action items (10 min)
- ADR updates with owner and due date
- Debt register updates
- Architectural risk mitigations
Format 4: Safety Retrospective (After Incidents)

The safety retro is not time-boxed to a calendar cadence — it is triggered by safety-relevant events: a guardrail bypass, a PII exposure, a significant refusal rate drop, or a hallucination that reached production users.
Blameless Safety Retro Principles

Safety incidents in GenAI systems are almost always systems failures, not individual failures. The blameless postmortem principle applies fully: the goal is to find the system gap, not to assign fault.
No named individuals in the root cause analysis — only system components, processes, and data.
"How did our system allow this?" not "Who let this happen?"
The red-team set is treated as incomplete, not as evidence that someone failed to do their job.
Every safety incident must produce at least one addition to the red-team eval set.
5-Why Analysis for GenAI Safety Events

## Safety Retro: 5-Why Template

**Incident:** [One sentence describing what happened]
**Date/Time:** [UTC timestamp]
**User impact:** [How many users affected, what they experienced]

### Timeline
| Time (UTC) | Event |
|---|---|
| 14:03 | Prompt change merged to main |
| 14:22 | Canary deployment to 5% traffic |
| 15:41 | First user report of policy violation |
| 15:49 | On-call paged |
| 16:02 | Rollback completed |

### 5-Why Analysis
**Why 1:** Why did the policy violation reach users?
→ The canary was at 5% and the safety fitness function did not catch the violation.

**Why 2:** Why did the safety fitness function not catch it?
→ The red-team set did not contain prompts similar to the user input that triggered the violation.

**Why 3:** Why did the red-team set not cover this case?
→ The red-team set was last updated 4 months ago and did not reflect the new user population segment.

**Why 4:** Why was the red-team set not updated when the user population changed?
→ There was no process to trigger a red-team set review when the product expanded to a new segment.

**Why 5:** Why was there no such process?
→ Root cause: the safety fitness function is treated as a fixed artifact rather than a living document tied to product changes.

### Action Items
1. Add the triggering user input (anonymized) to red-team set. Owner: @alice. Due: 2025-07-09.
2. Create process: any product expansion to a new user segment triggers a red-team set review.
   Owner: @bob. Due: 2025-07-16.
3. Add a coverage metric to the safety fitness function: % of known attack categories covered.
   Owner: @carol. Due: 2025-07-30.
Action Item Tracking Across All Formats

Action items from all four retro formats feed into the same tracking system. The key discipline is closing the loop — verifying at the next retro that action items from the previous retro were completed:

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date
from typing import Literal

RetroFormat = Literal["sprint", "quality", "architecture", "safety"]
ActionStatus = Literal["open", "in_progress", "done", "deferred", "cancelled"]

@dataclass
class RetroActionItem:
    retro_date: date
    retro_format: RetroFormat
    description: str
    owner: str
    done_when: str       # observable, verifiable completion criterion
    due_date: date
    status: ActionStatus = "open"
    completed_date: date | None = None
    completion_evidence: str = ""  # link to PR, ticket, or doc

    def is_overdue(self) -> bool:
        return self.status == "open" and date.today() > self.due_date

    def mark_done(self, evidence: str) -> None:
        self.status = "done"
        self.completed_date = date.today()
        self.completion_evidence = evidence

def open_retro(
    previous_action_items: list[RetroActionItem],
    retro_format: RetroFormat,
) -> None:
    """
    Begin every retro by reviewing open action items from previous retros.
    This creates accountability and surfaces systemic blockers.
    """
    overdue = [a for a in previous_action_items if a.is_overdue()]
    pending = [a for a in previous_action_items if a.status == "open" and not a.is_overdue()]
    done = [a for a in previous_action_items if a.status == "done"]

    print(f"Opening {retro_format} retro — action item status:")
    print(f"  Done:    {len(done)}")
    print(f"  Pending: {len(pending)}")
    print(f"  Overdue: {len(overdue)}")

    if overdue:
        print("\nOverdue items (discuss before new topics):")
        for item in overdue:
            print(f"  [{item.due_date}] {item.description} — owner: {item.owner}")
Choosing the Right Format
Situation
Recommended format
Why
End of every sprint
Sprint retro (4Ls)
Process and delivery rhythm — quick, regular calibration
Eval score has been drifting for 3+ weeks
Quality retro (data-led)
Requires 30-day chart and hypothesis generation, not a 60-min sprint retro
Team is considering moving to next architecture stage
Architecture retro (pre-mortem)
Needs ADR review, fitness function audit, and risk scenario generation
A guardrail bypass reached production
Safety retro (blameless + 5-why)
Safety incidents need dedicated time and a red-team addition before next deploy
Quarterly PI planning
Architecture retro + sprint retro combined
Architecture decisions feed into PI objectives; process items feed into sprint capacity
Summary
GenAI delivery teams need four retrospective formats, not one. Each serves a distinct purpose and requires a distinct data set:
Sprint retro (biweekly, 60 min): delivery process and immediate friction, anchored by eval trend and deployment metrics. Format: 4Ls.
Quality retro (monthly, 90 min): output quality trends, user feedback themes, and eval set coverage. Format: data-led, hypothesis → experiment.
Architecture retro (quarterly, 2 hours): ADR outcomes, fitness function gaps, debt register, and risk pre-mortem. Format: pre-mortem + structured review.
Safety retro (triggered, 60–90 min): blameless analysis of safety events. Format: timeline + 5-why. Always produces a red-team set addition.
All formats share the same output discipline: every action item has a single owner, a verifiable done-when criterion, and a due date before the next retro of the same format.
